speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, August the 27th, 1996. We are interviewing Abram Jachniuk. Abram, introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Rita Jachniuk. We got married in 1949, March the 26th. Thank God we're together until now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hope we'll be together for a long time. This is my grandson, Seth Klein. This is Jeffrey Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is mine-- my son, Jake-- Jake-- Jacob Jachniuk. You just mine daughter, Eda Klein. And this is my grandson-- EDA (DAUGHTER): Son-in-law. --Paul. Where are my-- I'm sorry. RITA (WIFE): No, your son-in-law. I'm sorry, this is my son-in-law. EDA (DAUGHTER): Mhm. Paul, Paul Klein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there anything that any of you would like to say about your father? EDA (DAUGHTER): I would, yes. First, I'd like to say that I've been hearing this story of the Holocaust from my father, through my father, ever since I can remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time, of course, the story got bigger. More was said. Things were discovered. Also, my father always tried to let us know about the family that we never got to meet, grandparents, aunts, uncles, cousins. And through him we got to know them as much as possible. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We have always, my brother and I, and my husband, of course, my mother, and my children, have always seen my father as a brave, courageous person. We have thought of him as a true hero, in the real sense of the word. Not only because of what he went through, not only because he survived these horrible things, but because of the type of person that he became afterwards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father is the kindest, gentlest person that I know. He is good to everybody. Of course, his family loves him very much and respects him. But so do friends, and just people whose lives he touches. He's just that type of a person. He did not allow these terrible things to embitter him. He always kept his faith. His spirit was always up. He's a hopeful person. And therefore, we're all very, very proud of him, especially his grandchildren. They cannot be here today because they're in school, at work. However, they are very proud of their zaide. And they look up to him. I also would like to just say one word of thanks to Steven Spielberg, of course, and to all the people involved in doing this. This is a true mitzvah. These stories need to be heard. Because reading them in history books will never be the same, as-- as hearing them from the people who actually experience this horror-- these horrors. And sometimes in history books, things get lost. Kids, a lot of times, feel that things are exaggerated. And of course, stories like these are looked upon as they couldn't possibly have happened that way. But when you hear it from people who went through it, I think-- I think it will-- it will live on. And hopefully will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your father's experience had any influence on the way you've tried to bring up your two sons?

speaker: interviewer
text: RITA (WIFE): Of course. I've always had a very strong feeling towards Judaism. Family has always been very, very important to me. And just-- you know, just knowing what the Jewish people went through just kind of strengthened my ties, and just-- just made my identity with the Jewish people stronger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyone else would like to say anything? RITA (WIFE): She said everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: JACOB (SON): Well, I'd just like to reaffirm what my sister just said, the wonderful things. I couldn't have put it better myself, other than just to go on record for my father, about how proud I am of him, for all the-- the years, and the upbringing. [CRYING] PAUL (SON-IN-LAW): You know, last summer I went to Poland. And I had the opportunity to visit Auschwitz and Birkenau camps, to witness, to see with your own eyes what has happened 50-some years ago. It was just almost unbearable, unthinkable that one man could do something like that to another. But to sit here and hear the story that Abe just told us, and to hear many, many other stories from other people who survived, it's just unthinkable, unimaginable that simply that could have happened and the world just stood by and-- and basically did very little to help these people. And I hope that the eyes of the next generation open up and this will be a lesson for many, many other people, other governments, so that at least they would not do something like that to another-- to another man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's just unbelievable to hear a story like that, and to be able to live with it for so many years. We're very, very proud that we have a member of our family that survived it and is able to tell everybody what has actually happened, and how much we love him and how much he brought to us, at least, the 24 years that we are married and this is our anniversary today. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Happy anniversary. PAUL (SON-IN-LAW): Thank you. EDA (DAUGHTER): Thank you. I have one more question for Abram. Is there any message that you want to give to your children, or to your grandchildren, with regard to the life that you've had to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say? I just want to say that I hope they don't have to get through what I-- I went-- this was my hope, that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I want to thank all of you. I particularly want to thank you, Abram, for telling your story, all of you for being here. Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. RITA (WIFE): Thank you. EDA (DAUGHTER): Thank you. RITA (WIFE): Thank you very much. I appreciate, very much. Because I always-- I always want to tell the-- the story, and I just couldn't do it. But with your help, I did it. And I'm very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, so [INAUDIBLE], Etia Blumer, with Aaron. And this is my brother, what is now in Israel. Yeah, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this picture for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Roza, with Jacob and Yossel with Jacob. In the belly was Jacob. This picture was taking in 1937, in Korost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [INAUDIBLE]. That's Jacob. [INAUDIBLE]. The picture was taken probably, the same time, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Korost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she must be from 19-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1917, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was about 20 years old when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this shows that-- that I have inquired as to a house, that somebody lives in that house. Until now, the house is still there. And I never went and I never-- never went to see anyone at that house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's written on that document?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's written that-- it says here, Abram Jachniuk, live in quarters until-- until-- 19-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in 1939. He has a house there. And every-- everything was taken by the Germans. And in that house, lives now one-- his name is her uncle, [INAUDIBLE]. He promised to pay me for that house. I never went for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a document that I got from that-- from Korost, that I'm-- I am, Abram Jachniuk, live until 1939, in Korost. And they took me, the Germans took me in ghetto. And all the family, what is there, all mine parents' brothers and sisters were there, here. They-- they were killed. And everything-- what, all the belongings they-- they took away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I-- this was in 1957. I-- I-- I wrote a letter to-- Korost, where I live, and they send me that. I ask them for that, and they send me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It came from the government? The Korost government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from Korost govern-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was there an organization that was doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's like-- how to tell? Like, a mayor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my son, Jacob Jachniuk, my brother, Monia Yachniuk. Mine daughter, Eda Klein. This is my brother's wife, Frejda.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I am here. And this is my wife, Frejda Jachniuk. And this is Mira, his sister, my sister-in-law. And he is mine-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you call it? Son-in-law. [LAUGHS] We were describing this picture from left to right. Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taking in-- I think it was taken here in America, in Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewee
text: EDA (DAUGHTER): [INAUDIBLE]? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on-- EDA (DAUGHTER): Jeffrey's. Oh, Jeffrey's bar mitzvah, on my grandson's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 19-- PAUL (SON-IN-LAW): That was what--

speaker: interviewee
text: EDA (DAUGHTER): '93? No, that's the first one.

speaker: interviewee
text: RITA (WIFE): '91. PAUL (SON-IN-LAW): '91. EDA (DAUGHTER): '91. 1971. RITA (WIFE): '91.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Are we ready?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is mine grandson, Seth, mine son-in-law, Paul Klein, my daughter, Eda Klein, and my grandson, Jeffrey Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1991. This is on his bar mitzvah, on Jeffrey's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Philadelphia. EDA (DAUGHTER): Ridgebury. Ridgebury.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going from left to right, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Jacob, my son, Jacob Jachniuk. My brother, [? Misha ?] [? Yachniuk. ?] And this is my wife, Frejda Yachniuk. This-- this is me, Abram Jachniuk. And here is Seth. RITA (WIFE): [? Shea. ?] Either [? Shea ?] or [? Shay ?] [INAUDIBLE]. RITA (WIFE): (WHISPERS) The bar mitzvah. And Frejda, Fredja Yachniuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to Frejda?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Eda ?] [INAUDIBLE] and [? Aaron ?] [INAUDIBLE], and Eda Klein, mine daughter, him and the son-in-law, Paul Klein, him and grandson, Jeff Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture of was take-- taking in-- EDA (DAUGHTER): Israel. Israel. JACOB (SON): Israel. PAUL (SON-IN-LAW): Israel. In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was mine nephew's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '90-- '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]
